narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite the challenges many face in this field, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that led to your application's rejection in a clear way. The admissions model looks at patterns from past students—like their early and later undergrad grades, LSAT scores, undergraduate GPA, family income, and whether they plan full-time studies—to predict the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try. For you, it calculated an 82.1% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which is high enough that the school automatically rejected your application to prioritize applicants predicted to succeed. The biggest factors pushing toward this failure prediction were your Year 1 and Year 3 grade deciles, both at the lowest level in your school—far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack standing—which had the strongest negative impact on the model's view of your bar exam odds. Your LSAT score of 35, a bit under the average of around 37, helped pull the prediction slightly toward passing, as did your undergraduate GPA of 2.90, which is close to but below the usual 3.21. Your middle family income bracket nudged things a little toward failure compared to higher-earning peers, while your plan for full-time enrollment offered a small positive boost, in line with most students. Overall, your early and later undergrad performance stood out as the most influential drags compared to typical applicants who rank higher in their classes and pass the bar more reliably. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many overcome similar starts through targeted effort. Next, I recommend focusing on boosting your academic deciles by excelling in remaining coursework, retaking the LSAT to aim above average, and seeking mentorship programs tailored for Black women in law to build a stronger profile. You could also explore schools with holistic reviews or gap-year improvements to reapply stronger. Keep pushing forward; your story is far from over.
